items.md: panels. According to Barks's voucher, he was paid for thirteen pages "plus 30 panels," which was evidently Western's method of paying him for drawing more than eight panels per page. The vouchers for a few other stories in this crowded format carry similar notations.
items.value: panels. According to Barks's voucher, he was paid for thirteen pages "plus 30 panels," which was evidently Western's method of paying him for drawing more than eight panels per page. The vouchers for a few other stories in this crowded format carry similar notations.

items.md: Changes: The last two panels of this story are by an artist other than Barks. In them, Donald learns that his adventures as a firebug have been only a bad dream. In Barks's original version, Donald went to jail for accidentally burning down a courthouse.

items.md: DONALD DUCK in "Seals Are So Smart!" - 10 - The nephews play ball at the beach with a baby seal, and this sets Donald to dreaming of how much money he could make with a trained seal act. *(Jan. 25, 1946)*

items.md: DONALD DUCK - 1 (inside front cover) - Donald tries on many hats and buys a huge sombrero *(Dec. 30, 1946)*
items.value: DONALD DUCK - 1 (inside front cover) - Donald tries on many hats and buys a huge sombrero (Dec. 30, 1946)

items.md: DONALD DUCK in "Volcano Valley" - 30 - Donald accidentally receives a full-size World War II bomber instead of the model plane he ordered, and sells it to the South American country of Volcanovia. The ducks are flown to Volcanovia, where they learn that they can leave the country only by becoming national heroes. Donald's efforts to become a national hero lead to his being proclaimed a national menace instead. *(Dec. 9, 1946)*

items.md: ## Four Color 159 - (August) 1947 - 52 pages
items.value: Four Color 159 - (August) 1947 - 52 pages

items.md: ### Donald Duck in the Ghost of the Grotto
items.value: Donald Duck in the Ghost of the Grotto

items.md: DONALD DUCK and the Ghost of the Grotto - 26 - While the ducks are running a kelp-gathering boat in the West Indies, they are told of the mystery of an armored man who appears every fifty years to kidnap a small boy. This time, the armored man kidnaps Dewey, and the ducks fight to free him from the armored man's stronghold in a grotto in a coral reef, beneath a rotten old galleon guarded by an enormous octopus. *(Apr. 15, 1947)*
items.value: DONALD DUCK and the Ghost of the Grotto - 26 - While the ducks are running a kelp-gathering boat in the West Indies, they are told of the mystery of an armored man who appears every fifty years to kidnap a small boy. This time, the armored man kidnaps Dewey, and the ducks fight to free him from the armored man's stronghold in a grotto in a coral reef, beneath a rotten old galleon guarded by an enormous octopus. (Apr. 15, 1947)

items.md: Barks said in the 1974 interview that this story was a rare example of a story that was conceived as ten pages for *Walt Disney's Comics* and grew into a longer story for *Donald Duck*. "I can remember the first idea I had on that was just trying to figure out something Donald could do. I thought of him sailing boats and came up with a potential ten pages of gathering seaweed, and selling this kelp, which would give me a lot of gags with boats. Like I told you before, I think of a scene, I mean a locale, and think, 'Well, I feel in the mood to draw boats, and the ocean, and so on,' and that would cause me to start working on that particular type of story. As I developed more and more things with the story, I think it's quite possible that that 'Ghost of the Grotto' was brought in as a menace. There is so much in that, I couldn't have thought of it in a whole bucketful of writing at once. It had to come out one thing after another."
items.value: Barks said in the 1974 interview that this story was a rare example of a story that was conceived as ten pages for Walt Disney's Comics and grew into a longer story for Donald Duck. "I can remember the first idea I had on that was just trying to figure out something Donald could do. I thought of him sailing boats and came up with a potential ten pages of gathering seaweed, and selling this kelp, which would give me a lot of gags with boats. Like I told you before, I think of a scene, I mean a locale, and think, 'Well, I feel in the mood to draw boats, and the ocean, and so on,' and that would cause me to start working on that particular type of story. As I developed more and more things with the story, I think it's quite possible that that 'Ghost of the Grotto' was brought in as a menace. There is so much in that, I couldn't have thought of it in a whole bucketful of writing at once. It had to come out one thing after another."

items.md: DONALD DUCK'S Christmas on Bear Mountain - 20 - Donald's stingy old Uncle Scrooge McDuck lets the ducks use his mountain cabin for the Christmas holidays, planning to test Donald's bravery by coming to the cabin disguised as a bear. However, two real bears — a mother and her cub — turn up at the cabin soon after the ducks' arrival. *(July 22, 1947)*

items.md: DONALD DUCK in "The Old Castle's Secret" - 32 - Uncle Scrooge and the ducks go to Scotland to find the treasure hidden in the walls of the old McDuck castle, and there they encounter a "ghost" that is invisible except for the shadow cast by its skeleton. The "ghost" soon steals the treasure and traps the ducks on a balcony a hundred feet above the ground, but the nephews get down safely and find a secret entrance to the castle through an old graveyard. *(Dec. 3, 1947)*.

items.caption: Barks holds his own copy of Donald Duck Four Color No. 189, 1948, the first comic book for which he illustrated the cover. The words stamped on the cover read as follows: "This comic is the personal file copy of Carl Barks. Anyone else possessing the book has stolen it." Comic-book cover © 1948 Walt Disney Productions.
